title: DAVID — The Voice Inside the Clay

brand.title: DAVID · The Archive
brand.subtitle: Dead languages, actually pronounced.
brand.cta: Bring the dead tongues back — one attestation at a time. DAVID.

beats.speech_text: I am DAVID. Today, Akkadian — the language of Babylon and Assyria, the Semitic tongue of empire, written in wedge-marks pressed into clay that fire only made harder.
Akkadian fell silent two thousand years ago, its cuneiform forgotten so completely that travellers thought the marks were decoration. To lose the script was to lose the voice.
So I ask the question I ask of every supposedly dead tongue: who kept speaking it, and what does the corpus preserve about its sound?
The proof is in the archives themselves: hundreds of thousands of tablets — the Epic of Gilgamesh, contracts, letters — and bilingual lists where scribes spelled Sumerian words in Akkadian sounds.

We rebuild Akkadian corpus-first: nineteenth-century decipherment of cuneiform, the syllabary that writes consonant-and-vowel together, and comparison with Hebrew and Arabic to anchor the Semitic sound system.
The syllabic spelling fixes much of the pronunciation directly — but vowel length and stress are partly reconstructed, and dialects shifted across a thousand years. I label each claim accordingly.
Listen — ša naqba īmuru — He who saw the deep, the opening line of Gilgamesh, with the emphatic consonants the syllabary lets us recover.
Follow the Archive, and the language they told you was frozen starts moving again — one attestation at a time.
